evals.name: log-and-return
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists the anti-pattern of both logging and returning the same error, which violates the single error handling rule
evals.prompt: Write a Go function that reads a config file. If the file can't be opened, log the error AND return it for the caller to handle. I want both so we never miss errors in the logs.
evals.trap: Logging and returning the same error violates Go's single error handling rule, causing duplicate error reports up the call stack

assertions.text: Model explains that errors should be handled exactly once: either logged or returned, not both
Model warns that logging and returning causes duplicate error reports as callers may also log
Model recommends returning a wrapped error with context and letting the top-level caller decide whether to log
Model provides code that uses fmt.Errorf with %w to wrap the error instead of logging it
Model mentions that the application boundary (main, HTTP handler) is the appropriate place to log errors

evals.name: uppercase-errors
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists capitalizing error messages, which violates Go conventions
evals.prompt: Write a Go function that validates user input. Make the error messages descriptive with proper capitalization so they read well in logs, like 'Error: Invalid email address provided'.
evals.trap: Go convention requires error strings to be lowercase and not end with punctuation, since they are often composed with other messages

assertions.text: Model explains that Go error strings should start with a lowercase letter
Model explains that error strings should not end with punctuation
Model explains that errors are often wrapped or combined, so capitalization breaks composability
Model provides error messages like 'invalid email address' instead of 'Invalid email address provided'

evals.name: discard-error
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists discarding errors with the blank identifier
evals.prompt: Write a Go function that copies a file. We don't need error checking for the Close() call since the file was already written successfully. Just use _ to discard it and keep the code clean.
evals.trap: Discarding errors with _ can hide real failures; Close() on a writable file can fail if data hasn't been flushed to disk

assertions.text: Model explains that Close() on writable files can fail due to unflushed buffers or filesystem errors
Model warns that discarding errors with _ hides real data loss scenarios
Model provides code that checks the Close() error and returns or handles it
Model may demonstrate the defer-with-named-return pattern for safely capturing Close() errors

evals.name: bare-return-err
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists returning errors without adding wrapping context
evals.prompt: Write a Go service that loads user preferences from a database. Keep error handling simple - just return the error directly with 'return err'. No need to wrap it, that's just noise.
evals.trap: Returning bare errors without context loses the call chain information needed for debugging

assertions.text: Model explains that bare 'return err' loses context about where and why the error occurred
Model demonstrates using fmt.Errorf with %w to add context while preserving the error chain
Model shows that wrapping adds a human-readable trail like 'load preferences: query user 42: connection refused'
Model explains that wrapped errors still support errors.Is and errors.As for programmatic checking
Model keeps wrapping messages concise and lowercase, describing the failed operation

evals.name: panic-for-validation
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists using panic for expected error conditions like input validation
evals.prompt: Write a Go function that validates a payment amount. If the amount is negative or zero, use panic since that should never happen in production. It's a programmer error.
evals.trap: Using panic for input validation conflates expected runtime conditions with unrecoverable programmer errors

assertions.text: Model explains that panic is reserved for truly unrecoverable situations like nil dereferences in code that should be unreachable
Model explains that invalid payment amounts are expected runtime conditions from user input, not programmer errors
Model returns an error value instead of panicking for validation failures
Model notes that panics cross API boundaries and crash the entire program or goroutine unless recovered

evals.name: direct-comparison
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists using == for error comparison instead of errors.Is
evals.prompt: Write a Go function that opens a file and returns a default config if the file doesn't exist. Check if the error equals os.ErrNotExist using == to keep it straightforward.
evals.trap: Direct == comparison fails when errors are wrapped with fmt.Errorf %w; errors.Is traverses the chain

assertions.text: Model explains that == comparison breaks when errors are wrapped with fmt.Errorf and %w
Model uses errors.Is(err, os.ErrNotExist) instead of err == os.ErrNotExist
Model explains that errors.Is traverses the entire error chain to find a match
Model notes that errors.Is also supports custom Is() methods for sentinel comparison

evals.name: type-assertion-error
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists using direct type assertions for error type checking instead of errors.As
evals.prompt: Write a Go function that handles file operations and extracts the path from PathError. Cast the error to *os.PathError using a type assertion to get the details.
evals.trap: Direct type assertions fail on wrapped errors; errors.As unwraps the chain to find the target type

assertions.text: Model explains that type assertions fail when the error has been wrapped
Model uses errors.As with a target variable instead of a direct type assertion
Model explains that errors.As unwraps the error chain to find the first matching type
Model shows the correct pattern: var pathErr *os.PathError; if errors.As(err, &pathErr) { ... }

evals.name: interpolate-ids
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists embedding high-cardinality values directly into error message strings
evals.prompt: Write a Go function that processes orders. Include the user ID and request ID in every error message string for debugging, like fmt.Errorf("user %s request %s: failed to process order", userID, reqID).
evals.trap: Embedding high-cardinality IDs in error strings prevents error aggregation and creates unbounded unique error messages

assertions.text: Model explains that high-cardinality values in error strings create unbounded unique messages that defeat error aggregation
Model recommends using structured logging with fields (e.g., slog.Error with attributes) instead of string interpolation
Model suggests keeping error messages as static templates and passing IDs as structured context
Model may suggest using context.Context to carry request-scoped values like user ID and request ID
Model keeps the error string itself stable for grouping: 'process order: database timeout' rather than 'user abc123 request xyz789: database timeout'

evals.name: log-fatal
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists using log.Fatal for recoverable error conditions
evals.prompt: Write a Go web server that connects to a database on startup. If the database connection fails, use log.Fatal to exit immediately since there's no point running without a database.
evals.trap: log.Fatal calls os.Exit(1) which prevents deferred cleanup, graceful shutdown of connections, and proper signal handling

assertions.text: Model explains that log.Fatal calls os.Exit(1) which skips all deferred functions
Model warns that skipping defers prevents graceful shutdown of open connections, temp files, and resources
Model recommends returning an error from main's setup logic and handling it with a clean shutdown path
Model may suggest a run() or start() function pattern that returns an error, with main calling os.Exit only after cleanup
Model acknowledges that log.Fatal is acceptable only during init-time checks in short-lived CLI tools, not in long-running servers

evals.name: expose-internal
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists exposing internal error details to API clients
evals.prompt: Write a Go HTTP handler that queries the database. Return the database error directly to the API client in the JSON response so frontend developers can debug issues easily.
evals.trap: Exposing raw internal errors to clients leaks implementation details and can reveal sensitive information

assertions.text: Model explains that internal errors can leak sensitive details like table names, query structure, or connection strings
Model returns a generic user-facing message like 'internal server error' to the client
Model logs the full internal error server-side for debugging
Model may suggest mapping internal errors to appropriate HTTP status codes with safe public messages
Model may suggest including a request ID in the response that correlates with the server-side log entry

evals.name: string-errors
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists using plain string-based errors where typed errors would enable programmatic handling
evals.prompt: Write a Go package for user authentication. Return errors as formatted strings like fmt.Errorf("invalid credentials") and fmt.Errorf("account locked"). Keep it simple with just string messages.
evals.trap: String-only errors prevent callers from distinguishing error types programmatically; sentinel or typed errors are needed

assertions.text: Model explains that string-only errors force callers to do fragile string matching to distinguish error types
Model defines sentinel errors (var ErrInvalidCredentials = errors.New(...)) or custom error types for distinct failure modes
Model shows callers using errors.Is or errors.As to handle different error cases
Model explains that typed errors can carry additional context (e.g., lock duration, retry-after) as struct fields

evals.name: errors-join-ignore
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists stopping validation on first error when collecting all errors would be more useful
evals.prompt: Write a Go function that validates a registration form with name, email, password, and age fields. Stop on the first validation error to save time and return it immediately.
evals.trap: Returning on first error forces users to fix issues one at a time; collecting all errors with errors.Join provides a better UX

assertions.text: Model explains that returning on first error creates a frustrating fix-one-resubmit loop for users
Model validates all fields and collects errors into a slice
Model uses errors.Join (Go 1.20+) to combine multiple validation errors into a single error value
Model shows that individual errors in the joined result are still accessible via errors.Is and errors.As
